critic_raw:
This CNN-LSTM versus logistic regression experiment for 30-day readmission prediction demonstrates good attention to temporal splitting, preprocessing discipline, and use of AUROC as a primary metric. The feature engineering is thoughtful, capturing temporal utilization patterns and clinical instability signals. However, several important methodological concerns remain.

The baseline logistic regression is trained on an 'identical feature set' as the CNN-LSTM, but this creates an unnatural comparison for logistic regression: features designed for sequential neural modeling (rolling window counts, time since last admission, admission frequency stability) are not the features a clinical practitioner would engineer for logistic regression. The appropriate comparison would use features best suited to each model type, or alternatively, features suited to logistic regression should be used for both, and then the CNN-LSTM's advantage from learning additional temporal structure should be demonstrated. As designed, the comparison may underestimate logistic regression's performance relative to its natural feature space.

The temporal split allocates 1,500 patients to validation (first half of year 4) and 2,500 to test (second half of year 4 and all of year 5), while training covers years 1-3 (~6,000 patients). The asymmetric test set spanning both year 4.5 and year 5 is unusual: it conflates a near-term generalization period with a far-term one, potentially masking degradation over time if the model's performance declines significantly from year 4.5 to year 5. Time-stratified reporting within the test window would be more informative.

The CNN-LSTM architecture's 1D convolutional component is described as extracting 'local patterns in visit dynamics,' but with irregular visit intervals and visits spanning a 180-day lookback, applying fixed-kernel 1D convolutions over sequences of arbitrary clinical events (which lack fixed spatial or temporal regularity) may not be conceptually appropriate. The motivation for CNN over pure LSTM or temporal attention is not empirically validated through an ablation.

The success threshold of >=0.03 absolute AUROC improvement is presented as a meaningful criterion, but no power calculation is provided to establish that the study is adequately powered to detect this difference with the available ~2,500 test patients and ~15-25% readmission rate.
all_issues_raised: Logistic regression is evaluated on features designed for sequential neural models, potentially underestimating its performance relative to its natural feature space
Test set spans year 4.5 through year 5, conflating near-term and far-term generalization without time-stratified reporting within the test window
1D CNN over irregularly-spaced clinical event sequences is conceptually questionable; no ablation validates the CNN component's contribution versus LSTM alone
Success threshold of >=0.03 AUROC is asserted without a power calculation to confirm the study can reliably detect this difference
180-day lookback window for feature construction is applied uniformly but patients with fewer than 180 days of history are not discussed; potential selection bias toward established patients
1D CNN applied to clinical event sequences assumes translational invariance not present in temporal clinical data — architectural rationale is not justified
180-day look-back features for validation/test patients span the training period boundary; preprocessing parameters fitted on training data may not represent look-back window data for these patients
Success criterion of ≥0.03 AUROC improvement is not grounded in clinical utility analysis or established literature — appears arbitrary
Feature aggregation strategy for logistic regression baseline from sequential data is unspecified, creating an undocumented design choice that affects baseline performance
No comparison to a pure LSTM baseline to isolate whether the CNN component adds value over recurrent modeling alone

Temporal features engineered for logistic regression baseline (rolling admission counts, time-since-last-admission) replicate part of the CNN-LSTM's sequential modeling, partially confounding the architectural comparison
180-day lookback for test-set patients near the split boundary crosses into the training window; no explicit statement that this cross-window access is leakage-free
Success criterion of ≥0.03 absolute AUROC is not grounded in a power analysis; with ~2,500 test patients this difference may not be statistically significant
Mean imputation without missingness indicators may cause models to miss informative patterns in missing data, particularly for chronic disease patients who drop out of care
No ablation comparing CNN-only or LSTM-only submodels to isolate whether temporal convolution, recurrence, or their combination provides the performance benefit
all_issues_adjudicated: Logistic regression is evaluated on features designed for sequential neural models, potentially underestimating its performance relative to its natural feature space
